revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]][[fr:Controverses sur le sanctuaire Yasukuni]][[ja:靖国神社問題]][[ko:야스쿠니 신사 논쟁]][[zh:靖國神社爭議]]
{{Short description|Controversies of a Shinto shrine in Japan|qq=}} {{Use mdy dates|date=December 2013|qq=}} [[File:Yasukuni_Shrine_2012.JPG|thumb|Yasukuni Shrine; March 2012]] There are a number of controversies relating to {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} and its war museum {{ill|Yūshūkan|en|Yūshūkan|qid=Q2855971|lt=Yūshūkan|1=Yūshūkan}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, Japan. The shrine is based on {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, as opposed to traditional Japanese Shinto, and has a close history with [[:en:Statism in Shōwa Japan|Statism in Shōwa Japan]]. Most of the dead served the Emperors of Japan during wars from 1867 to 1951 but they also include civilians in service and government officials. It is the belief of Shinto that Yasukuni enshrines the actual souls of the dead, known as kami in Japanese. The kami are honoured through liturgical texts and ritual incantations known as Norito. However, of the 2,466,532 men named in the shrine's ''Book of Souls'', 1066 are {{ill|Japanese war crimes|en|Japanese war crimes|qid=Q701321|lt=war criminals|1=Japanese war crimes}} convicted by the {{ill|International Military Tribunal for the Far East|en|International Military Tribunal for the Far East|qid=Q133539310|lt=International Military Tribunal for the Far East|1=International Military Tribunal for the Far East}}, following {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}: Eleven men were convicted of Class A war crimes, one was charged with Class A but found guilty of lesser Class B war crimes, and a further two men were charged with Class A but died before they could be convicted.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e2321.html |title=Yasukuni Shrine |access-date=March 23, 2008 |work=japan-guide.com|qq=}}</ref> Because of the decision to honour individuals who were found responsible for serious breaches of {{ill|international humanitarian law|en|international humanitarian law|qid=Q381148|lt=international humanitarian law|1=International humanitarian law}}, {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, {{ill|Russia|en|Russia|qid=Q159|lt=Russia|1=Russia}},<ref>{{cite web|url=https://view.inews.11.com/k/20221021A0561900?web_channel=wap&openApp=false&f=newdc|title=俄罗斯外交部发言人扎哈罗娃批判日本岸田首相向靖国神社供奉"真榊"祭品|date=Nov 9, 2022|language=zh|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.mid.ru/ru/foreign_policy/news/1822249/?TSPD_101_R0=08765fb817ab20004f6d7268295685fc9ff062e96f112871023bbb6113b1f65941eab952d9d47df308df656e6114300012c5190b24cfb575b85b6a8a881d527012228727c5d38790cf61c7290d26763bfb41106e2ff8470129b271120642a753|title=Заявление МИД России об ответных мерах в отношении членов Палаты представителей Парламента Японии|date=July 15, 2022|qq=}}</ref> {{ill|South Korea|en|South Korea|qid=Q884|lt=South Korea|1=South Korea}}, and {{ill|North Korea|en|North Korea|qid=Q423|lt=North Korea|1=North Korea}} have called the Yasukuni Shrine an exemplar of the {{ill|Japanese nationalism|en|Japanese nationalism|qid=Q1331440|lt=nationalist|1=Japanese nationalism}}, {{ill|Japanese history textbook controversies|en|Japanese history textbook controversies|qid=Q708992|lt=revisionist|1=Japanese history textbook controversies}}, and unapologetic approach Japan has taken towards its conduct during World War II. This has made visits to the shrine by {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Japanese prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}s, {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=cabinet members|1=Cabinet of Japan}}, or {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=parliamentarians|1=National Diet}} extremely controversial. Former {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} {{ill|Junichiro Koizumi|en|Junichiro Koizumi|qid=Q130852|lt=Junichiro Koizumi|1=Junichiro Koizumi}} made annual personal non-governmental visits from 2001 to 2006. Since 1985, China, North Korea, and South Korea have protested such visits. The decision as to who is enshrined at Yasukuni remains a religious activity. The practice started when State Shinto was controlled and managed by the civilian and then military governments of {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Imperial Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}. The post-war [[Government of Japan|governments of Japan]] have continued to uphold this {{ill|Separation of church and state|en|Separation of church and state|qid=Q753623|lt=legal separation|1=Separation of church and state}}. The Yasukuni priesthood have complete religious autonomy over deciding whom they bestow enshrinement. It is thought that enshrinement is permanent and irreversible by the current [[Kannushi]]. ==Release of documents== On March 29, 2007, a book of documents was released by Japan's National Diet Library called "A New Compilation of Materials on the Yasukuni Shrine Problems"<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20070329a5.html |title=Paper: Yasukuni, state in '69 OK'd war criminal inclusion | the Japan Times Online |access-date=April 13, 2008 |archive-date=July 13, 2012 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120713071905/http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20070329a5.html |url-status=dead |qq=}}</ref> including declassified documents from the Occupational Government, the Japanese Health and Welfare Ministry and Yasukuni Shrine. The documents purportedly draw a connection between the Japanese Government and the war criminal enshrinement.<ref>http://www.korea.net/News/News/LangView.asp?serial_no=20070329019&lang_no=1&part=110&SearchDay={{dead link|date=November 2016 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes |qq=}}</ref> According to Prime Minister {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|qid=Q132345|lt=Shinzo Abe|1=Shinzo Abe}} the government had no say in who is enshrined.<ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.bloomberg.com/apps/news?pid=20601101&sid=aRX1E8Q02OqM | work=Bloomberg | title=Japan Didn't Push for Yasukuni to Honor War Criminals (Update4) | date=March 29, 2007|qq=}}</ref> In addition, Vice Minister of Health, Labor, and Welfare Tsuji Tetsuo told reporters that the former ministry was "in charge of keeping the personal records of soldiers and civilian employees of the military, and with presenting records as the need arose."<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.csis.org/media/csis/pubs/0702qjapan_china.pdf |title=Archived copy |access-date=May 3, 2009 |archive-date=April 18, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090418210301/http://www.csis.org/media/csis/pubs/0702qjapan_china.pdf |url-status=dead |qq=}}</ref> "A New Compilation of Materials on the Yasukuni Shrine Problems"<ref>{{cite web |url=http://lccn.loc.gov/2007553672 |title=Library of Congress LCCN Permalink 2007553672 |website=lccn.loc.gov |lccn=2007553672 |access-date=22 May 2022 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120712040232/http://lccn.loc.gov/2007553672 |archive-date=12 July 2012 |url-status=dead|qq=}}</ref> has been entered into the Library of Congress. ==War criminals== The shrine enshrines and, according to [[Shinto]] beliefs, provides a permanent residence for the spirits of those who have fought on behalf of the emperor, regardless of whether they died in combat. 1,066 of the enshrined [[kami]] were {{ill|Prisoner of war|en|Prisoner of war|qid=Q179637|lt=POW|1=Prisoner of war}}s convicted of some level of war crime after World War II and a further two were charged with war crimes but died before their trials were completed. Enshrinement typically carries absolution of earthly deeds. One of the criteria for enshrinement at Yasukuni is that a person be listed as having died of any cause while on duty in the war dead registry of the Japanese government. According to documents released on March 28, 2007, by the National Diet Library of Japan, Health and Welfare Ministry officials and Yasukuni representatives agreed during a meeting, on January 31, 1969, that Class-A war criminals judged at the {{ill|Tokyo Trial|en|Tokyo Trial|qid=Q320680|lt=Tokyo Trial|1=Tokyo Trial}} were "able to be honored" as decided by the Shrine Priests and decided not to make this decision public.<ref>The Japan Times (March 29, 2007) [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20070329a5.html Yasukuni, state in '69 OK'd war criminal inclusion] (June 1, 2007)</ref> On October 17, 1978, fourteen men who had been charged with Class A war crimes—eleven were convicted as Class A war criminals, one was convicted of Class B, two died before completing trial—were enshrined as {{nihongo|"Martyrs of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}}"|昭和殉難者|Shōwa junnansha|qq=}} because they were on the war dead registry: *Death by hanging: *:{{ill|Hideki Tojo|en|Hideki Tojo|qid=Q160847|lt=Hideki Tōjō|1=Hideki Tojo}}, {{ill|Seishirō Itagaki|en|Seishirō Itagaki|qid=Q704374|lt=Seishirō Itagaki|1=Seishirō Itagaki}}, {{ill|Heitarō Kimura|en|Heitarō Kimura|qid=Q712861|lt=Heitarō Kimura|1=Heitarō Kimura}}, {{ill|Kenji Doihara|en|Kenji Doihara|qid=Q74296|lt=Kenji Doihara|1=Kenji Doihara}}, {{ill|Akira Mutō|en|Akira Mutō|qid=Q607868|lt=Akira Mutō|1=Akira Mutō}}, {{ill|Kōki Hirota|en|Kōki Hirota|qid=Q351652|lt=Kōki Hirota|1=Kōki Hirota}} *:{{ill|Iwane Matsui|en|Iwane Matsui|qid=Q353901|lt=Iwane Matsui|1=Iwane Matsui}} (sentenced for only Class B offenses) *Lifetime imprisonment: *:{{ill|Yoshijirō Umezu|en|Yoshijirō Umezu|qid=Q472566|lt=Yoshijirō Umezu|1=Yoshijirō Umezu}}, {{ill|Kuniaki Koiso|en|Kuniaki Koiso|qid=Q315549|lt=Kuniaki Koiso|1=Kuniaki Koiso}}, [[:en:Kiichirō Hiranuma|Kiichirō Hiranuma]], {{ill|Toshio Shiratori|en|Toshio Shiratori|qid=Q717308|lt=Toshio Shiratori|1=Toshio Shiratori}} *20-year imprisonment: *:{{ill|Shigenori Tōgō|en|Shigenori Tōgō|qid=Q509796|lt=Shigenori Tōgō|1=Shigenori Tōgō}} *Died before a judicial decision was reached (owing to illness or disease): *:{{ill|Osami Nagano|en|Osami Nagano|qid=Q553670|lt=Osami Nagano|1=Osami Nagano}}, [[:en:Yosuke Matsuoka|Yosuke Matsuoka]] All imprisoned war criminals either had their sentences commuted or were released by 1958. The enshrinement was revealed to the media on April 19, 1979, and a still-ongoing controversy started in 1985. ==Yūshūkan War Museum== [[File:Yokosuka D4Y1 at the Yūshūkan.jpg|thumb|Yokosuka D4Y1 at the Yūshūkan War Museum]] Yasukuni Shrine operates a war museum of the {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|qid=Q130436|lt=History of Japan|1=History of Japan}} (the {{ill|Yūshūkan|en|Yūshūkan|qid=Q2855971|lt=Yūshūkan|1=Yūshūkan}}), which some observers<ref>{{cite web | url=http://www.shanghaidaily.com/Opinion/foreign-perspectives/Yushukan-museum-whitewashes-wartime-atrocities/shdaily.shtml | title=Yushukan museum whitewashes wartime atrocities | date=January 16, 2014 |qq=}}</ref> have criticized as presenting a [[:en:historical revisionism (political)|revisionist]] interpretation. A documentary-style propaganda video shown to museum visitors portrays Japan's conquest of {{ill|East Asia|en|East Asia|qid=Q27231|lt=East Asia|1=East Asia}} during the pre-World War II period as an effort to save the region from the imperial advances of colonial {{ill|Western world|en|Western world|qid=Q160381|lt=Western powers|1=Western world}}. The museum has no mention of any of the atrocities committed by the {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|en|Imperial Japanese Army|qid=Q276535|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}}, including the {{ill|Nanjing Massacre|en|Nanjing Massacre|qid=Q192055|lt=Nanjing Massacre|1=Nanjing Massacre}}. ==Izokukai== The political overtones of Yasukuni Shrine are attributed to 4 factors. One is the ideology of State Shinto, which regarded any wars waged in the name of the emperor as just and anyone who died fighting for the emperor as an {{nihongo|''eirei''|英霊||hero spirit|qq=}}. A second is that Japanese Class-A war criminals are honored at the Shrine and more importantly - that their wartime genocidal, crimes against humanity, etc. criminal actions are absolved of culpability. Another factor is the influence of various support organizations, especially the {{ill|Japan War-Bereaved Families Association|en|Japan War-Bereaved Families Association|qid=Q3662025|lt=Japan War-Bereaved Families Association|1=Japan War-Bereaved Families Association}} ({{nihongo|Izokukai|遺族会|qq=}}), the largest organization representing the families of war dead from World War II. The most critical issue with many Asian nations that were invaded by Japan is that the Shrine was a meeting place for the Japanese elite to stir-up a frenzy among the Japanese public, before their military went on what was routinely a war of merciless conquest over what they considered ''inferior'' peoples and that genocide was a central component in that effort. Yasukuni Shrine considers the Izokukai as its ''de facto'' lay organization. The Izokukai was formerly known as the {{nihongo|Izoku Kōsei Renmei|遺族厚生連盟||War-Dead-Family Welfare Union|qq=}}, established in 1947. The original purpose of the Izoku Kōsei Renmei was "pursuing the end of warfare, establishing global peace and world prosperity and contributing to the welfare of the humanity." They sought "to provide relief and assistance to the families of those who died in the (Asia Pacific) war ". The organization provided assistance to the widows, orphans and aging parents of deceased veterans as well as lobbying the government on behalf of those families' interests. However, in 1953 the organization became a trust foundation and changed its name to Izokukai. The organization changed its main purpose to pursue "the establishment of a peaceful Japan, the cultivation of character, and the promotion of morality" and to "seek to praise ''eirei'', to promote the welfare of the families of the war dead, and to seek recognition and compensation for civilian auxiliary units." The change, which removed the mention of international pacifism and inserted a reference to ''eirei'', is regarded as giving a nationalist slant to the character of the organization. Chairmen of the organization have usually been members of the governing Liberal Democratic party and the organization is regarded as the informal pipeline between the LDP and the Shrine. In 1962, {{ill|Okinori Kaya|en|Okinori Kaya|qid=Q1037034|lt=Okinori Kaya|1=Okinori Kaya}}, a militant member of the LDP and a convicted class A war criminal, was appointed chairman. ==Politicians' visits== Japanese politicians' visits to worship at Yasukuni Shrine have resulted in controversy. This issue first surfaced when {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Emperor Hirohito|1=Hirohito}} refused to visit the shrine from 1978 until his death in 1989.<ref name="JT1">{{cite web|url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fd20060820pb.html |title=Notes on Yasukuni and a week that will live in infamy |access-date=April 13, 2008 |author=Brasor, Philip |date=August 20, 2006 |work={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|qq=}}</ref> According to a memorandum released in 2006 kept by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} [[:en:Grand Steward|Grand Steward]] Tomohiko Tomita, Hirohito stated that the reason he stopped visiting the shrine was because of the decision to enshrine class A war criminals.<ref name="JT2">{{cite web|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/07/21/national/hirohito-visits-to-yasukuni-stopped-over-war-criminals/ |title=Hirohito visits to Yasukuni stopped over war criminals |access-date=January 5, 2020 |date=July 21, 2006 |work={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|qq=}}</ref> [[File:Junichiro Koizumi (cropped) during arrival ceremony on South Lawn of White House.jpg|thumb|150px|left|Former Japanese Prime Minister {{ill|Junichiro Koizumi|en|Junichiro Koizumi|qid=Q130852|lt=Junichiro Koizumi|1=Junichiro Koizumi}}]] Prime Minister {{ill|Junichiro Koizumi|en|Junichiro Koizumi|qid=Q130852|lt=Junichiro Koizumi|1=Junichiro Koizumi}} was one of the most outspoken and controversial visitors. On October 17, 2005, Koizumi visited the shrine for the fifth time since taking office. Although he claimed that his visit was a private affair, it came only days before {{ill|Minister for Foreign Affairs (Japan)|en|Minister for Foreign Affairs (Japan)|qid=Q1355043|lt=Japanese Foreign Minister|1=Minister for Foreign Affairs (Japan)}} {{ill|Nobutaka Machimura|en|Nobutaka Machimura|qid=Q697258|lt=Nobutaka Machimura|1=Nobutaka Machimura}} was scheduled to visit Chinese officials in {{ill|Beijing|en|Beijing|qid=Q956|lt=Beijing|1=Beijing}} to strengthen Sino-Japanese relations. The [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] responded by canceling the scheduled visit as they consider the shrine a glorification of Japan's past military aggression.<ref name=cancel>{{cite web|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/getarticle.pl5?nn20051019a1.htm |title=China cancels meeting with Machimura |access-date=April 13, 2008 |date=October 19, 2005 |work={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|qq=}}</ref> Koizumi's annual visits continued to draw criticism from around the world. During the 2005 [[:en:APEC|APEC]] summit in {{ill|Busan|en|Busan|qid=Q16520|lt=Busan|1=Busan}}, South Korea, Chinese Foreign Minister {{ill|Li Zhaoxing|en|Li Zhaoxing|qid=Q463697|lt=Li Zhaoxing|1=Li Zhaoxing}} blasted Koizumi's visits, asking, "What would European people think if German leaders were to visit (memorials) related to Hitler and Nazis?"<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2005/11/16/national/china-playing-the-hitler-card/#.WIdO6_krLIU |title=China playing the Hitler card |access-date=April 13, 2008 |date=November 16, 2005 |work={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|qq=}}</ref> In 2006, {{ill|Henry Hyde|en|Henry Hyde|qid=Q3132849|lt=Henry Hyde|1=Henry Hyde}}, chairman of the {{ill|United States House Committee on Foreign Affairs|en|United States House Committee on Foreign Affairs|qid=Q5973577|lt=United States House Committee on Foreign Affairs|1=United States House Committee on Foreign Affairs}}, stated that Koizumi would embarrass the {{ill|United States Congress|en|United States Congress|qid=Q11268|lt=United States Congress|1=United States Congress}} and offend American veterans of World War II if he were to give a Congressional speech after making another visit to Yasukuni.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20060517a1.html |title=Bid to address Congress has Yasukuni proviso |access-date=April 13, 2008 |date=May 17, 2006 |work={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}} |author=Nakata, Hiroko|qq=}}</ref> Koizumi's expected successor, {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|qid=Q132345|lt=Shinzo Abe|1=Shinzo Abe}}, visited the shrine in April 2006 before he took office. Although this visit concerned both Chinese and South Korean governments,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://asia.news.yahoo.com/060804/kyodo/d8j9m1ko0.html |title=China expresses concern over reported Abe visit to Yasukuni |access-date=April 13, 2008 |date=August 4, 2006 |work=[[Yahoo! News]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080622132708/http://asia.news.yahoo.com/060804/kyodo/d8j9m1ko0.html |archive-date=June 22, 2008 |url-status=dead |df=mdy |qq=}}</ref> Abe remained vague as to whether he had visited or would visit the shrine in the future. Subsequent events have led some to suggest that a compromise on the issue was reached with China.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.uofaweb.ualberta.ca/chinainstitute/news.cfm?story=51368 |title=Japan, China reach agreement over shrine |access-date=April 13, 2008 |date=October 6, 2006 |author=York, Geoffrey |work=[[University of Alberta]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080222025644/http://www.uofaweb.ualberta.ca/chinainstitute/news.cfm?story=51368 |archive-date=February 22, 2008 |url-status=dead |df=mdy-all |qq=}}</ref> Abe publicly supported his predecessor's visits to the shrine, and he made at least one visit to the shrine during his term as prime minister.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/asia-pacific/4392480.stm |title=Profile: Shinzo Abe |access-date=April 13, 2008 |date=September 12, 2007 |publisher={{ill|BBC News|en|BBC News|qid=Q1160945|lt=BBC News|1=BBC News}}|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.scmp.com/news/asia/article/1399087/japan-wants-talks-china-korea-yasukuni-shrine|title = Japan wants talks with China, Korea on Yasukuni Shrine|date = January 6, 2014|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2013/dec/27/chinese-media-attacks-shinzo-abe-yasukuni-shrine |title=This page has been removed | News | the Guardian |website=[[TheGuardian.com]] |access-date=December 14, 2016 |archive-date=February 2, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170202113715/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2013/dec/27/chinese-media-attacks-shinzo-abe-yasukuni-shrine |url-status=dead |qq=}}</ref> On June 7, 2007, former {{ill|President of the Republic of China|en|President of the Republic of China|qid=Q887003|lt=President of the Republic of China|1=President of the Republic of China}} {{ill|Lee Teng-hui|en|Lee Teng-hui|qid=Q315528|lt=Lee Teng-hui|1=Lee Teng-hui}} visited the Shrine to pay tribute to his older brother who died in the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Navy|Japanese Imperial Navy]]; he too volunteered as a [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] officer. Former prime minister {{ill|Yasuo Fukuda|en|Yasuo Fukuda|qid=Q101137|lt=Yasuo Fukuda|1=Yasuo Fukuda}} has vowed never to visit the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chinadaily.net/world/2007-09/16/content_6110119.htm |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120730153538/http://www.chinadaily.net/world/2007-09/16/content_6110119.htm |url-status=dead |archive-date=July 30, 2012 |title=Fukuda leads Japan PM race, won't visit Yasukuni |access-date=April 13, 2008 |date=September 16, 2007 |work=[[China Daily]] |qq=}}</ref> Fukuda's open political opposition to the shrine has helped improve relations with China, and North and South Korea.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.economist.com/daily.columns/asiaview/PrinterFriendly.cfm?story_id=10909449 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20130122033048/http://www.economist.com/daily.columns/asiaview/PrinterFriendly.cfm?story_id=10909449 |url-status=dead |archive-date=January 22, 2013 |title=All quiet on the eastern front? |access-date=April 13, 2008 |date=February 26, 2008 |newspaper={{ill|The Economist|en|The Economist|qid=Q180089|lt=The Economist|1=The Economist}}|qq=}}</ref> A group of 62 Diet members from the Liberal Democratic Party and the People's New Party, including former {{ill|Minister of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries (Japan)|en|Minister of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries (Japan)|qid=Q8191055|lt=Minister of Agriculture|1=Minister of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries (Japan)}} {{ill|Yoshinobu Shimamura|en|Yoshinobu Shimamura|qid=Q11476388|lt=Yoshinobu Shimamura|1=Yoshinobu Shimamura}} and Fukuda's special adviser {{ill|Eriko Yamatani|en|Eriko Yamatani|qid=Q534539|lt=Eriko Yamatani|1=Eriko Yamatani}}, visited the shrine on April 22, 2008.<ref name="search.japantimes.co.jp">''Lawmakers visit Yasukuni festival'', http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20080423a5.html</ref> On April 23, 2013, a group of 169 Japanese lawmakers visited the shrine.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://news.xinhuanet.com/english/indepth/2013-04/23/c_132333083.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130428060313/http://news.xinhuanet.com/english/indepth/2013-04/23/c_132333083.htm|url-status=dead|archive-date=April 28, 2013|title=Commentary: Japanese lawmakers' visit to notorious shrine exposes cowardice of politicians|access-date=April 23, 2013|date=April 23, 2013|publisher=xinhuanet.com|author=Wang Haiqing|qq=}}</ref> General opinions in Japan of the politicians' visits have varied significantly, with the percentages of those who approve ranging from as low as 38% in telephone surveys to as high as 71% on the internet.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://blogos.com/outline/76988/|title=首相の靖国参拝 朝日の世論調査でも6割賛成|website=BLOGOS|language=ja|access-date=2019-05-24|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.sankeibiz.jp/express/news/140107/exa1401071016000-n1.htm|title=内閣支持率５０％回復 改憲や靖国参拝 若い世代が評価 産経新聞社・ＦＮＮ世論調査|website=SankeiBiz（サンケイビズ）|language=ja-JP|access-date=2019-05-24|qq=}}</ref> In a 2015 self-administered survey by {{ill|Genron NPO|en|Genron NPO|qid=Q11631016|lt=Genron NPO|1=Genron NPO}}, 66% of the respondents did not see a problem with the visits while 15.7% opposed them completely.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.genron-npo.net/pdf/2015forum_en.pdf|title=11th Japan-China Joint Opinion Poll Analysis Report (page 32)|qq=}}</ref> A 2006 public opinion poll taken by ''[[Nihon Keizai Shimbun]]'' also found that half of the respondents supported Koizumi's visit to the shrine while 37% opposed it.<ref>{{Cite |date=2024-09-04 |title=Sergi Korsensky, the Ukrainian ambassador to Japan, visited the Yasukuni Shrine in Tokyo, where Clas.. |url=https://www.mk.co.kr/en/world/11109931 |access-date=2024-09-05 |website=[[Maeil Business Newspaper]] |language=en|qq=}}</ref>{{Unreliable source?|reason=The English version of this website uses automatic machine translation; see [[WP:KO/RS]]|date=November 2024|qq=}} ==Political impact== The controversial nature of the shrine has figured largely in both domestic Japanese politics and relations with other Asian countries. The controversy has been reignited nearly every year since 1975, when {{ill|prime minister|en|prime minister|qid=Q14212|lt=prime minister|1=Prime minister}} {{ill|Takeo Miki|en|Takeo Miki|qid=Q317675|lt=Takeo Miki|1=Takeo Miki}} visited the shrine as a private individual on August 15, the day that Japan commemorates the end of World War II. The next year, his successor [[:en:Fukuda Takeo|Fukuda Takeo]] visited as a private individual yet signed the visitors' book as prime minister. Several other Japanese {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime ministers|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} have visited the shrine since 1979: [[:en:Masayoshi Ohira|Masayoshi Ohira]] in 1979; [[:en:Zenko Suzuki|Zenko Suzuki]] in 1980, 1981 and 1982; {{ill|Yasuhiro Nakasone|en|Yasuhiro Nakasone|qid=Q250828|lt=Yasuhiro Nakasone|1=Yasuhiro Nakasone}} in 1983 and 1985 (on the latter occasion, he offered flowers which had been paid for with government money); {{ill|Kiichi Miyazawa|en|Kiichi Miyazawa|qid=Q315565|lt=Kiichi Miyazawa|1=Kiichi Miyazawa}} in 1992, which visit was kept secret until 1996 (he had paid a visit in 1980 before becoming Prime Minister); {{ill|Ryutaro Hashimoto|en|Ryutaro Hashimoto|qid=Q309374|lt=Ryutaro Hashimoto|1=Ryutaro Hashimoto}} in 1996; and {{ill|Junichiro Koizumi|en|Junichiro Koizumi|qid=Q130852|lt=Junichiro Koizumi|1=Junichiro Koizumi}}, who visited six times (2001, 2002, 2003, 2004, 2005, and 2006). Visits by Japanese prime ministers to the shrine have resulted in official condemnation by neighbouring countries since 1985, as they see it as an attempt to legitimize {{ill|Japanese militarism|en|Japanese militarism|qid=Q702868|lt=Japan's past militarism|1=Japanese militarism}}. Visits to the shrine are also controversial in the domestic debate over the proper role of religion in Japanese government. Some {{ill|Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)|en|Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)|qid=Q232595|lt=Liberal Democratic Party (LDP)|1=Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)}} politicians insist that visits are protected by the constitutional right to freedom of religion and that it is appropriate for legislators to pay their respects to those fallen in war. However, proposals for the construction of a [[:en:secular|secular]] memorial, so that those wishing to honor Japan's military dead do not have to visit Yasukuni, have thus far failed, ostensibly for technical details rather than the rejection of a secular memorial. The Japanese government conducts yearly memorial services to commemorate the War in {{ill|Nippon Budokan|en|Nippon Budokan|qid=Q386246|lt=Budokan|1=Nippon Budokan}} ("Martial Arts Hall", a secular building) which is near Yasukuni shrine, so that the attendees can later visit Yasukuni Shrine privately if they so wish. On his first visit to Japan since leaving office in February 2003, former {{ill|President of South Korea|en|President of South Korea|qid=Q6296418|lt=South Korean President|1=President of South Korea}} [[:en:Kim Dae Jung|Kim Dae Jung]] openly criticised Japanese politicians' visits to the shrine, and proposed that the 14 Class A war criminals be moved to a different location. He said, "If that option is realized, I will not express opposition to visits to Yasukuni Shrine (by Koizumi or other Japanese leaders)". Kim noted that Koizumi promised at a meeting in {{ill|Shanghai|en|Shanghai|qid=Q8686|lt=Shanghai|1=Shanghai}} in 2001 to consider building a new memorial facility that could replace Yasukuni Shrine and enable anyone to worship there without hesitation. The government of the People's Republic of China has been the most vocal critic of the shrine and some Japanese observers have suggested that the issue of Yasukuni Shrine is just as heavily tied to China's internal politics as it is to the historical conduct of Japan's military and the perceived degree of its remorse for its actions. They state that tolerance on the part of {{ill|Chinese Communist Party|en|Chinese Communist Party|qid=Q17427|lt=Chinese Communist Party|1=Chinese Communist Party}} authorities for large-scale public protests in mainland China against the shrine contrasts strongly with the authority exercised against any kind of domestic political dissent.<ref>[https://www.sydney.edu.au/arts/about/our-people/academic-staff/james-reilly.html Reilly, James. (2012). ''Strong Society, Smart State: The Rise of Public Opinion in China's Japan Policy.'' New York: Columbia University Press.] {{ISBN|9780231158060|qq=}}; Chen Weiss, Jessica. (2014). ''[https://www.oxfordscholarship.com/view/10.1093/acprof:oso/9780199387557.001.0001/acprof-9780199387557 Powerful Patriots: Nationalist Protest in China's Foreign Relations].'' New York: Oxford University Press. {{ISBN|9780199387557|qq=}}</ref> == Historic meaning and controversy == Yasukuni Shrine is a privately owned Shinto shrine located in the heart of Tokyo, Japan. The Meiji Emperor built the shrine in 1886 to house the remains and souls of those who died in civil conflicts.<ref>{{Cite web|author=MARI YAMAGUCHI|agency=Associated Press|title=Japan's Suga makes offering at war shrine but doesn't visit|url=https://www.heraldextra.com/news/world/japans-suga-makes-offering-at-war-shrine-but-doesnt-visit/article_c4dba5bc-ce4d-5e76-a11c-383eb71802ae.html|access-date=2021-05-18|website=Daily Herald|language=en|archive-date=May 18, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210518161237/https://www.heraldextra.com/news/world/japans-suga-makes-offering-at-war-shrine-but-doesnt-visit/article_c4dba5bc-ce4d-5e76-a11c-383eb71802ae.html|url-status=dead|qq=}}</ref> The shrine is now the memorial site for over 2.5 million people who have died in conflict, mainly in World War II.  The inclusion of 14 convicted Class-A war criminals in the shrine has resulted in controversy, particularly after the visits of Japanese prime ministers. China and at times South Korea have also objected to the shrine, as prior to World War II, Japan controlled the Korean Peninsula and parts of the Manchurian region in China and committed the {{ill|Nanjing Massacre|en|Nanjing Massacre|qid=Q192055|lt=massacre of Nanjing|1=Nanjing Massacre}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Fallows|first=James|date=2014-01-11|title=What Is a 'Class-A War Criminal'? More on the Yasukuni Controversy|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/international/archive/2014/01/what-is-a-class-a-war-criminal-more-on-the-yasukuni-controversy/283004/|access-date=2021-05-18|website=The Atlantic|language=en|qq=}}</ref> "Class-A" war criminals are those who are charged with "crimes against peace" while Class-C criminals are charged with crimes against humanity. In some cases, requests by survivors and their families to have their names removed from the Yasukuni have been denied, leading to litigation. Koreans and Taiwanese who were forced to fight in the war are also listed on the Yasukuni as deity of Japan, some of whom survived the war, and Japanese from Okinawa who were forced to self-determine during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|qid=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} are also at issue.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://apjjf.org/-Nobumasa-Tanaka/2744/article.html|title=Desecration of the Dead: Bereaved Okinawan Families Sue Yasukuni to End Relatives' Enshrinement|website=The Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus|date=May 3, 2008 |qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.yna.co.kr/view/AKR20110722002600004|script-title=ko:야스쿠니 합사취소訴 생존원고 김희종씨|date=July 22, 2011|website=[[Yonhap News Agency]]|qq=}}</ref> Japanese courts have rejected these lawsuits several times. Numerous requests have been made by groups in other countries to remove the remains of the 14 war criminals or their own family members from the shrine, but as the shrine is owned by a private religious institution rather than the government, and the removal of remains would violate Shinto beliefs, the requests were denied.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Hirakawa |first=Sukehiro |date=2017-07-04 |title=At Yasukuni Shrine, People Pray for the Dead; Leave Politics Out of It |url=https://japan-forward.com/at-yasukuni-shrine-people-pray-for-the-dead-leave-politics-out-of-it/ |access-date=2021-05-18 |website=[[Japan Forward]] |language=en-US|qq=}}</ref> In Shinto, a body cannot be removed once it is placed into a shrine to be worshipped as a "kami". This shows that Yasukuni is a different facility from the national cemetery where bereaved families have religious freedom and veto rights. A group claiming to represent [[:en:Taiwanese aborigine|Taiwanese aborigine]]s led by politician {{ill|Kao Chin Su-mei|en|Kao Chin Su-mei|qid=Q697862|lt=Kao Chin Su-mei|1=Kao Chin Su-mei}} attempted to visit Yasukuni Shrine with the sponsorship of the Japan Catholic Council for Justice and Peace. Their intention was to peacefully request the removal of their relatives from the shrine, and to pray for the return of their ancestors' souls. Request to perform religious rites within the Yasukuni property were refused and they were blocked from entering Yasukuni by Japanese protesters and police. A demonstration was organized by a group of more than one hundred {{ill|Uyoku dantai|en|Uyoku dantai|qid=Q181924|lt=Japanese nationalists|1=Uyoku dantai}} to block them from the shrine and prevent them from performing spirit-calling religious rituals within the property the Shrine objected. Japanese police allowed the protesters to remain on the grounds because their entrance to the shrine was not objected by the shrine; however they blocked the Taiwanese from leaving their buses, citing measures to prevent clashes between the two groups. After about an hour and a half, the Taiwanese group gave up their attempt. Kao Chin Su-mei and her group reportedly received death threats related to their visit, prompting the Taiwanese government to request Japanese authorities ensure her safety while in Japan.<ref>[http://www.chinapost.com.tw/taiwan/detail.asp?ID=63832&GRP=B Lawmaker and aborigines forbidden to visit Yasukuni]</ref> ==Debate in Japan== One controversy of political visits to the shrine is the constitutionality of visits by the Prime Minister. In the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}}, the {{ill|Separation of church and state|en|Separation of church and state|qid=Q753623|lt=separation of state and religion|1=Separation of church and state}} is explicit. Because the clause was written for the express purpose of preventing the return of State Shinto, many question the constitutionality of the Prime Minister visiting Yasukuni Shrine. Often the first question Japanese prime ministers are asked by journalists after a visit is, "Are you here as a private person or as Prime Minister?" In addition, whether the Prime Minister has signed the visitors' book indicating the position of signatory as {{nihongo|''shijin''|私人||private person|qq=}} or {{nihongo|''shushō''|首相||Prime Minister|qq=}} is diligently reported. All Prime Ministers have so far stated that their visit was private. However, although some leave the signature section blank or sign it as ''shijin'', others sign it as ''shushō''. The issue is somewhat different than that of visits by the German Chancellor to the Holocaust Memorial, which are explicitly made in the context of a state visit. Prime Minister Koizumi gave a somewhat cryptic answer, stating that he visited the shrine as Junichiro Koizumi, the Prime Minister of Japan. Some consider such statement as a move towards making visits somewhat official; others consider that it is pointing out that the whole issue of ''shijin'' vs ''shushō'' is somewhat meaningless. Some journals and news reports, such as one made by Kyodo News Agency on August 15, 2006, question whether in the case of Koizumi's visits, which are consistently claimed by Koizumi to be private, can be considered individual in nature when they are part of a campaign pledge, which in nature is political. Currently, most of the Japanese public and most jurists have agreed that there have been no constitutional violations yet. In 2014, Shinzo Abe, 15 of the 18 members of his cabinet, and 289 of 480 Diet members were affiliated to the openly {{ill|Historical revisionism|en|Historical revisionism|qid=Q874803|lt=revisionist|1=Historical revisionism}} lobby {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|en|Nippon Kaigi|qid=Q11507216|lt=Nippon Kaigi|1=Nippon Kaigi}}, which advocates the restoration of {{ill|Monarchism|en|Monarchism|qid=Q216669|lt=monarchy|1=Monarchism}} and {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, negates Japanese war crimes, and recommends the revision of the Constitution and school textbooks as well as visits by prime ministers to the Shrine.<ref name="2014 reshuffle">"[http://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/article/article.aspx?aid=2994558 Abe's reshuffle promotes right-wingers]" (Korea Joongang Daily – 2014/09/05)</ref><ref name=kato>{{cite news |author=Norihiro Kato |title=Tea Party Politics in Japan |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/09/13/opinion/tea-party-politics-in-japan.html |newspaper=New York Times |date=September 12, 2014|qq=}}</ref> The chief priest of the shrine, Yasuhisa Tokugawa (also the great-grandson of [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]], the last shōgun), is a member of Nippon Kaigi's representative committee.<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref> The views expressed by Yasukuni Shrine through its museum and website are also controversial. Both sites make it clear that Yasukuni Shrine does not regard the conduct of Japan during World War II as an act of aggression but rather a matter of self-defence and a heroic effort to repel [[:en:European imperialism|European imperialism]]. Defenders of (private) visits by the Prime Minister point out that, regardless, there is no other venue to pay respect to the fallen in Japan, so that the Prime Minister as well as the large number of Japanese who visit the shrine have no choice. Moreover, most people (including the Prime Minister) who visit Yasukuni deliberately avoid entering the museum so that the visit remains religious rather than political. A number of proposals have been made to alleviate controversy. One is to somehow "remove" the controversial spirits and place them in a different location so that visits to Yasukuni Shrine would not be as politically charged. This proposal has been strongly pushed by China and Korea. The Japanese government cannot force Yasukuni Shrine to do so (owing to the separation of church and state). Moreover, the shrine is adamant that once a kami has been housed at the shrine, it cannot be separated. The one method which is suggested as theologically valid is to abolish the entire enshrinement, then repeat the entire enshrinement rite of kami since the Boshin War without including the A class war criminals. Some argue that selective abolishment of enshrinement is technically possible, as there are several precedents of selective de-enshrinement in the Tokugawa era. The Shinto processes of ''[[bunrei]]'' and ''[[kanjō]]'' exist specifically to remove a kami from its shrine and re-enshrine it elsewhere, but typically leave the kami at the originating shrine intact and unchanged.<ref>{{ill|Karen Ann Smyers|en|Karen Ann Smyers|qid=Q6369485|lt=Smyers, Karen Ann|1=Karen Ann Smyers}}. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589|qq=}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029|qq=}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475]</ref> Another proposal is to create a separate secular memorial where the prime minister can make official state visits for memorial purposes. Critics point out that groups representing families of the war dead express no interest in such a memorial, preferring Yasukuni Shrine. Furthermore, the Japanese government already conducts yearly secular commemoration services at the Budokan for the families of soldiers killed in World War II. Afterwards, these families usually make private visits to Yasukuni Shrine, which is located within walking distance. Since the proposed memorial site is geographically distant, were the ceremony to be relocated to the proposed memorial site such visits would be made more difficult. A number of families of the names listed at the shrine have indicated that the controversy is disturbing the peaceful rest of their dead family members and that they wish to pay homage to them without controversy and media attention. There is in fact a memorial to the Japanese (unidentified) war dead within walking distance of Yasukuni, called {{nihongo|{{ill|Chidorigafuchi National Cemetery|en|Chidorigafuchi National Cemetery|qid=Q2972676|lt=Chidorigafuchi National Cemetery|1=Chidorigafuchi National Cemetery}}|千鳥ヶ淵戦没者墓苑|qq=}}, which has been suggested could be used as an alternative by Japanese politicians to pay their respects to those who died during the war. ==Textbook controversy== In May 2005, in the aftermath of {{ill|2005 anti-Japanese demonstrations|en|2005 anti-Japanese demonstrations|qid=Q82567|lt=anti-Japanese protests|1=2005 anti-Japanese demonstrations}} over the [[:en:Japanese history textbooks controversy|Japanese history textbooks controversy]], Chinese Vice-Premier {{ill|Wu Yi (politician)|en|Wu Yi (politician)|qid=Q50952|lt=Wu Yi|1=Wu Yi (politician)}} cut short her visit to Japan and flew home before a planned meeting with Japanese prime minister {{ill|Junichiro Koizumi|en|Junichiro Koizumi|qid=Q130852|lt=Junichiro Koizumi|1=Junichiro Koizumi}}. This was widely interpreted as a reaction to a statement by Koizumi the day before Wu's arrival that foreign countries should not interfere in Japan's domestic affairs, including the Yasukuni issue. Wu's visit was meant to improve strained relations between the two countries following the textbook controversy, and she had planned to ask Koizumi to stop his visits to the shrine. ==Prime ministerial visits== ===Junichiro Koizumi=== Prime Minister {{ill|Junichiro Koizumi|en|Junichiro Koizumi|qid=Q130852|lt=Junichiro Koizumi|1=Junichiro Koizumi}} made widely publicized annual visits to the shrine while in office. The official position of the Japanese government was that he visited as an individual citizen "to express respect and gratitude to the many people who lost their lives in the war," and not for the sake of gratifying war criminals or to dispute the results of the {{ill|International Military Tribunal for the Far East|en|International Military Tribunal for the Far East|qid=Q133539310|lt=International Military Tribunal for the Far East|1=International Military Tribunal for the Far East}}.<ref>{{cite web|title=Basic Position of the Government of Japan Regarding Prime Minister Koizumi's Visits to Yasukuni Shrine|url=http://www.mofa.go.jp/policy/postwar/yasukuni/position.html|publisher=Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Japan|access-date=December 26, 2013|qq=}}</ref> Koizumi made his final visit as prime minister on August 15, 2006, shortly before his retirement.<ref>http://mdn.mainichi-msn.co.jp/national/news/20060815p2a00m0na001000c.html {{Dead link|date=July 2010|qq=}}</ref> Officials in the [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] responded to Koizumi's 2005 visit by canceling a scheduled visit to China by {{ill|Minister for Foreign Affairs (Japan)|en|Minister for Foreign Affairs (Japan)|qid=Q1355043|lt=Japanese Foreign Minister|1=Minister for Foreign Affairs (Japan)}} {{ill|Nobutaka Machimura|en|Nobutaka Machimura|qid=Q697258|lt=Nobutaka Machimura|1=Nobutaka Machimura}} in protest.<ref name=cancel/> Koizumi's visits also provoked negative reactions in the United States. {{ill|Henry Hyde|en|Henry Hyde|qid=Q3132849|lt=Henry Hyde|1=Henry Hyde}}, a World War II veteran serving as the Republican chairman of the U.S. House of Representatives Committee on International Relations, wrote a letter to House Speaker Dennis Hastert in May 2006 urging that Koizumi only be allowed to speak at the U.S. Capitol if he agreed not to visit Yasukuni on the following anniversary of Japan's surrender.<ref>{{cite news|last=Nakata|first=Hiroko|title=Bid to address Congress has Yasukuni proviso|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2006/05/17/news/bid-to-address-congress-has-yasukuni-proviso/#.Ur2eQNxdUXE|access-date=December 27, 2013|newspaper={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|date=May 17, 2006|qq=}}</ref> Hyde's Democratic counterpart {{ill|Tom Lantos|en|Tom Lantos|qid=Q160717|lt=Tom Lantos|1=Tom Lantos}} also pressed for an end to the visits, stating that "paying one's respect to war criminals is morally bankrupt and unworthy of a great nation such as Japan."<ref>{{cite news|last=Sheldrick|first=Aaron|title=U.S. Lawmakers Urge Next Japan PM to Avoid Yasukuni|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/apps/news?pid=newsarchive&sid=arbbcpF0OhQA|access-date=December 26, 2013|newspaper=Bloomberg News|date=September 15, 2006|qq=}}</ref> ===Shinzo Abe=== Koizumi's successor {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|qid=Q132345|lt=Shinzo Abe|1=Shinzo Abe}} visited the shrine several times before and after his first stint as prime minister, but did not visit at all during his first term as prime minister from September 2006 to September 2007. Abe not visiting the shrine prompted a Japanese nationalist named Yoshihiro Tanjo to cut off his own little finger in protest and mail it to the LDP.<ref>[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/nn20070824a7.html Severed pinkie sent to LDP to protest Abe's Yasukuni no-show] ''{{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}'', August 24, 2007</ref> In April 2007, he made a ceremonial offering to the shrine, but did not actually visit himself. According to official reports the offering was made by Abe as a private citizen rather than in an official capacity, although it was reported that the card attached to the floral offering was signed Prime Minister Shinzo Abe [http://www.iht.com/articles/2007/05/08/news.japan.php]{{dead link|date=June 2024|bot=medic|qq=}}{{cbignore|bot=medic|qq=}}. Although Abe publicly supported his predecessor's visits to the shrine he did not visit the shrine himself during his term in office.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/asia-pacific/4392480.stm|title=Profile: Shinzo Abe|date=September 12, 2007|via=news.bbc.co.uk|qq=}}</ref> In August 2007, the 16 members of Abe's cabinet all declared they had no intention of visiting the shrine on the anniversary of the [[:en:Japanese surrender|Japanese surrender]]. Abe, who at this point had not disclosed whether he himself intended to go, commented "Paying homage at the Yasukuni temple, or not, is up to the individual, even for a Cabinet member. I expect people to use their own discretion."<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.japannewsreview.com/politics/politics/20070813page_id=1435|title=Abe on Yasukuni: 'To pay homage or not is up to the individual' - Japan News Review|date=February 20, 2012|access-date=August 8, 2018|archive-date=February 20, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120220064351/http://www.japannewsreview.com/politics/politics/20070813page_id=1435|url-status=bot: unknown|qq=}}</ref> {{ill|Sanae Takaichi|en|Sanae Takaichi|qid=Q1705028|lt=Sanae Takaichi|1=Sanae Takaichi}}, minister in charge of gender equality and Okinawa-related issues, ultimately visited the shrine in an apparent effort to avoid a rare absence of all Cabinet members at Yasukuni on the anniversary of Japan's official World War II surrender.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://home.kyodo.co.jp/modules/fstPhotos/index.php?photoid=23235 |title=State minister Takaichi visits Yasukuni Shrine |access-date=April 13, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080618070412/http://home.kyodo.co.jp/modules/fstPhotos/index.php?photoid=23235 |archive-date=June 18, 2008 |url-status=dead |df=mdy-all |qq=}}</ref> While campaigning for the presidency of the LDP in 2012, Abe said that he regretted not visiting the shrine while prime minister. He again refrained from visiting the shrine during the first year of his second stint as prime minister in consideration for improving relations with China and Korea, whose leaders refused to meet with Abe during this time. He said on December 9, 2013, that "it is natural that we should express our feelings of respect to the war dead who sacrificed their lives for the nation... but it is my thinking that we should avoid making [Yasukuni visits] political and diplomatic issues." In lieu of visiting, Abe sent ritual offerings to the shrine for festivals in April and October 2013, as well as the anniversary of the end of World War II in August 2013.<ref>{{cite news|title=Abe unlikely to visit Yasukuni by year-end in consideration for ties with neighbors |url=http://mainichi.jp/english/english/newsselect/news/20131225p2a00m0na009000c.html |access-date=December 26, 2013 |newspaper={{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|en|Mainichi Shimbun|qid=Q1136866|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|1=Mainichi Shimbun}} |date=December 25, 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131227003151/http://mainichi.jp/english/english/newsselect/news/20131225p2a00m0na009000c.html |archive-date=December 27, 2013 |df=mdy |qq=}}</ref> On May 19, 2013, Prime Minister Shinzo Abe said in an interview with {{ill|Foreign Affairs|en|Foreign Affairs|qid=Q1135577|lt=Foreign Affairs|1=Foreign Affairs}} that Yasukuni was comparable to {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|qid=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} and that it was natural to visit. The United States responded on October 3 of the same year, when U.S. Secretary of Defense {{ill|Chuck Hagel|en|Chuck Hagel|qid=Q381670|lt=Chuck Hagel|1=Chuck Hagel}} and U.S. Secretary of State {{ill|John Kerry|en|John Kerry|qid=Q22316|lt=John Kerry|1=John Kerry}} visited {{ill|Chidorigafuchi National Cemetery|en|Chidorigafuchi National Cemetery|qid=Q2972676|lt=Chidorigafuchi National Cemetery|1=Chidorigafuchi National Cemetery}}, noting that Chidorigafuchi is the most similar facility in Japan to Arlington Cemetery.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://japan.kantei.go.jp/tyoukanpress/201310/03_p.html|title=Press Conference by the Chief Cabinet Secretary Thursday, October 3, 2013|website=japan.kantei.go.jp|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bangkokpost.com/world/372813/kerry-at-japan-arlington-in-us-war-shrine-nudge|title=Kerry at 'Japan's Arlington' in US war shrine nudge|agency=AFP |date=2013-10-03|newspaper=Bangkok Post |archive-date=2023-11-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231109045433/www.bangkokpost.com/world/372813/kerry-at-japan-arlington-in-us-war-shrine-nudge |url-status=dead|qq=}}</ref> Abe's first visit to the shrine and {{ill|Chinreisha|en|Chinreisha|qid=Q2963793|lt=Chinreisha|1=Chinreisha}} as prime minister took place on December 26, 2013, the first anniversary of his second term in office. It was the first visit to the shrine by a sitting prime minister since Junichiro Koizumi visited in August 2006. Abe said that he "prayed to pay respect for the war dead who sacrificed their precious lives and hoped that they rest in peace," and said he had "no intention to neglect the feelings of the people in China and South Korea.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kantei.go.jp/foreign/96_abe/statement/201312/1202986_7801.html|title=Statement by Prime Minister Abe – Pledge for everlasting peace|publisher=[[Prime Minister's Official Residence (Japan)]]|access-date=December 26, 2013|qq=}}</ref>" The Chinese government published a protest that day, calling government visits to the shrine "an effort to glorify the Japanese militaristic history of external invasion and colonial rule and to challenge the outcome of World War II."<ref>{{cite news|title=Japanese prime minister visits Yasukuni war shrine |url=http://mainichi.jp/english/english/newsselect/news/20131226p2g00m0dm032000c.html |access-date=December 26, 2013 |newspaper=Associated Press |date=December 26, 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131227012900/http://mainichi.jp/english/english/newsselect/news/20131226p2g00m0dm032000c.html |archive-date=December 27, 2013 |df=mdy |qq=}}</ref> Chinese Ambassador to Japan, Cheng Yonghua, stated in an article published in the {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|en|Mainichi Shimbun|qid=Q1136866|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|1=Mainichi Shimbun}} that "Japanese leaders visiting the Yasukuni Shrine concerns their understandings of the aggressive war's nature and responsibility, which absolutely can not be accepted by the Chinese side." The ''Mainichi Shimbun'' argued in an editorial that the visit could "cast a dark shadow" on relations with the United States and other countries in addition to China and Korea.<ref>{{cite news|title=Abe's Yasukuni visit could cast dark shadow on Japanese foreign diplomacy |url=http://mainichi.jp/english/english/newsselect/news/20131226p2a00m0na004000c.html |access-date=December 26, 2013 |newspaper={{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|en|Mainichi Shimbun|qid=Q1136866|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|1=Mainichi Shimbun}} |date=December 26, 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131227013006/http://mainichi.jp/english/english/newsselect/news/20131226p2a00m0na004000c.html |archive-date=December 27, 2013 |df=mdy |qq=}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=S. Korea condemns Abe's 'anachronistic' visit to war shrine|url=https://www.google.com/hostednews/afp/article/ALeqM5iN5dETZYDJbPLehEgReTAt-g8vVQ?hl=en&docId=6bca182e-8249-4ef0-84e5-9d772b7b404c&index=0|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140305070835/http://www.google.com/hostednews/afp/article/ALeqM5iN5dETZYDJbPLehEgReTAt-g8vVQ?hl=en&docId=6bca182e-8249-4ef0-84e5-9d772b7b404c&index=0|url-status=dead|archive-date=March 5, 2014|access-date=27 December 2013|newspaper=AFP|date=26 December 2013|qq=}} {{cite news|title=Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe visits controversial Yasukuni Shrine for war dead|url=http://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/world/rest-of-world/Japanese-Prime-Minister-Shinzo-Abe-visits-controversial-Yasukuni-Shrine-for-war-dead/articleshow/27946781.cms|access-date=26 December 2013|newspaper=Times Of India|date=2013-12-26|qq=}} {{cite web|title=Посещение японским премьером храма Ясукуни вызывает сожаление у Москвы|url=http://ria.ru/world/20131226/986687478.html|publisher=Риановости|date=26 December 2013|access-date=26 December 2013|qq=}}</ref> The U.S. government criticized the prime minister on December 12, expressing disappointment that he went ahead with the visit despite Vice President Joe Biden's request that he not visit the Yasukuni Shrine.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.straitstimes.com/asia/biden-asked-abe-not-to-visit-yasukuni-war-shrine-says-report|title=Biden asked Abe not to visit Yasukuni war shrine, says report|date=January 29, 2014|newspaper=The Straits Times|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.foxnews.com/politics/us-disappointed-by-japanese-pms-visit-to-war-shrine|title=US 'disappointed' by Japanese PM's visit to war shrine|date=March 25, 2015|website=Washington Examiner|qq=}}</ref> On December 27, 2013, {{ill|Catherine Ashton|en|Catherine Ashton|qid=Q105667|lt=Catherine Ashton|1=Catherine Ashton}}, the EU's foreign policy chief, released a statement saying that the move does nothing to improve relations with its neighbors.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.tokyofoundation.org/research/detail.php?id=291|title=The Implications of Abe's Yasukuni VIsit | Research|website=The Tokyo Foundation for Policy Research|qq=}}</ref> On December 29, the Singapore government expressed regret over the Japanese prime minister's visit to Yasukuni Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.mfa.gov.sg/Newsroom/Press-Statements-Transcripts-and-Photos/2013/12/MFA-Spokesmans-Comments-in-response-to-media-queries-on-Japanese-Prime-Minister-Shinzo-Abes-visit-to|title=MFA Spokesmans Comments in response to media queries on Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abes visit to|website=www.mfa.gov.sg|qq=}}</ref> On January 19, 2014, Taiwanese president {{ill|Ma Ying-jeou|en|Ma Ying-jeou|qid=Q19216|lt=Ma Ying-jeou|1=Ma Ying-jeou}} wrote a post on Facebook denouncing shrine visits as rubbing salt in the wound.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://focustaiwan.tw/politics/201401190024|title=Ma condemns Japanese leaders' visit to war shrine - Focus Taiwan|date=January 19, 2014|website=Focus Taiwan - CNA English News|qq=}}</ref> As Abe visited the shrine, China's UN Ambassador {{ill|Liu Jieyi|en|Liu Jieyi|qid=Q15916890|lt=Liu Jieyi|1=Liu Jieyi}} said in taking the issue to the UN: "It all boils down to whether the leader of a country should stand on the side of maintaining the principles and purposes of the charter of the UN or to side with war criminals. The question inevitably arises as to what Abe is up to, where does he intend to take his country? The international community should remain vigilant and issue a warning ... that Abe must correct his erroneous outlook of history, he must correct his mistakes and he must not slip further down the wrong path." In response, Japan's UN Ambassador Motohide Yoshikawa said: "Abe visited Yasukuni Shrine to pay his respects and pray for the souls of the war dead and renew the pledge that Japan shall never again wage war. It was nothing more and nothing less."<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2014/1/9/china-takes-japan-shrine-dispute-to-un|title=China takes Japan shrine dispute to UN|website=Al Jazeera|qq=}}</ref> ===Other prime ministers=== In the wake of Abe's first term, his successor {{ill|Yasuo Fukuda|en|Yasuo Fukuda|qid=Q101137|lt=Yasuo Fukuda|1=Yasuo Fukuda}} vowed never to visit the shrine, a commitment applauded by Japan's Asian neighbors.<ref>[https://archive.today/20120730153538/http://www.chinadaily.net/world/2007-09/16/content_6110119.htm Fukuda leads Japan PM race, won't visit Yasukuni]</ref> Fukuda's open political opposition to the shrine led to improved relations with China, North and South Korea [https://archive.today/20130122033048/http://www.economist.com/daily.columns/asiaview/PrinterFriendly.cfm?story_id=10909449]. However, a group of 62 Diet members from the Liberal Democratic Party and the People's New Party, including former farm minister {{ill|Yoshinobu Shimamura|en|Yoshinobu Shimamura|qid=Q11476388|lt=Yoshinobu Shimamura|1=Yoshinobu Shimamura}} and {{ill|Eriko Yamatani|en|Eriko Yamatani|qid=Q534539|lt=Eriko Yamatani|1=Eriko Yamatani}}, a special adviser to Fukuda, visited the shrine on April 22, 2008.<ref name="search.japantimes.co.jp"/> The {{ill|Democratic Party of Japan|en|Democratic Party of Japan|qid=Q200314|lt=Democratic Party of Japan|1=Democratic Party of Japan}} governments between 2009 and 2012 also avoided the shrine. {{ill|Yukio Hatoyama|en|Yukio Hatoyama|qid=Q131077|lt=Yukio Hatoyama|1=Yukio Hatoyama}} pledged not to visit so long as war criminals are enshrined there.<ref>{{cite news|last=Wakabayashi|first=Daisuke|title=Hatoyama Pledges Not to Visit Yasukuni Shrine|url=https://www.wsj.com/news/articles/SB125001588907223315|access-date=December 26, 2013|newspaper=Wall Street Journal|date=August 12, 2009|qq=}}</ref> {{ill|Naoto Kan|en|Naoto Kan|qid=Q47846|lt=Naoto Kan|1=Naoto Kan}} ordered the entire cabinet not to visit the shrine on the anniversary of Japan's surrender, and said he had no plans to visit personally.<ref>{{cite news|last=Blair|first=Gavin|title=Japan PM Kan sends signal to Asian neighbors by shunning Yasukuni Shrine|url=http://www.csmonitor.com/World/Asia-Pacific/2010/0816/Japan-PM-Kan-sends-signal-to-Asian-neighbors-by-shunning-Yasukuni-Shrine|access-date=December 26, 2013|newspaper=Christian Science Monitor|date=August 16, 2010|qq=}}</ref> {{ill|Yoshihiko Noda|en|Yoshihiko Noda|qid=Q57647|lt=Yoshihiko Noda|1=Yoshihiko Noda}} stated in his first press conference that his cabinet would continue the policy of not making official visits.<ref>{{cite news|title=Press Conference by Prime Minister Yoshihiko Noda|url=http://www.kantei.go.jp/foreign/noda/statement/201109/02kaiken_e.html|access-date=December 26, 2013|date=September 2, 2011|qq=}}</ref> In 2022, {{ill|Fumio Kishida|en|Fumio Kishida|qid=Q359480|lt=Fumio Kishida|1=Fumio Kishida}} sent an offering to the shrine, but did not visit himself.<ref>{{cite news|title=Japan ministers visit war shrine as South Korea calls for end to historical tensions|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/aug/15/japan-ministers-visit-controversial-war-shrine-as-south-korea-calls-for-end-to-historical-tensions|access-date=April 15, 2023|date=August 15, 2022|qq=}}</ref> On April 21, 2023, Prime Minister Kishida sent a religious offering to the site.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Japan PM Kishida sends ritual offering to war-linked Yasukuni shrine |url=https://english.kyodonews.net/news/2023/04/1f7d6650531f-update1-japan-pm-kishida-sends-ritual-offering-to-war-linked-yasukuni-shrine.html |access-date=22 April 2023|date=21 April 2023 |website=Kyodo News|qq=}}</ref> ==Foreign dignitary visits== In June 2007, former Taiwanese president {{ill|Lee Teng-hui|en|Lee Teng-hui|qid=Q315528|lt=Lee Teng-hui|1=Lee Teng-hui}} visited the shrine to pay his respects to his elder brother Lee Teng-ching ({{lang|zh-tw|李登欽|qq=}}, or Lí Teng-khim in [[:en:Taiwanese Minnan|Taiwanese]] {{ill|Pe̍h-ōe-jī|en|Pe̍h-ōe-jī|qid=Q559173|lt=POJ|1=Pe̍h-ōe-jī}}), also known as Takenori Iwasato {{Nihongo||岩里武則||qq=}} in Japanese, who is among the 27,863 Taiwanese honored there.<ref name=lee1>[http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/asia-pacific/6729067.stm Taiwan ex-leader in shrine visit], BBC News (June 7, 2007)</ref> ==Emperor Shōwa's visits== [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] did not visit Yasukuni from 1978 until his death. His son, [[:en:Emperor Akihito|Emperor Akihito]], has not visited the shrine since becoming emperor (and has since continued after {{ill|2019 Japanese imperial transition|en|2019 Japanese imperial transition|qid=Q44023338|lt=his abdication in 2019|1=2019 Japanese imperial transition}}), instead choosing to send a lesser member of the royal household. {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Emperor Naruhito|1=Naruhito}} (Shōwa's grandson) does not visit the shrine, and sends a lesser member of the royal house as his father did. On July 20, 2006, ''[[Nihon Keizai Shimbun]]'' front-paged an article about the discovery of a memorandum detailing the reason Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Hirohito|1=Hirohito}} stopped visiting Yasukuni. The memorandum, kept by former chief of {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} Tomohiko Tomita, confirms for the first time the enshrinement of [[:en:Class A War Criminal|Class A War Criminal]]s such as [[:en:Yosuke Matsuoka|Yosuke Matsuoka]] and {{ill|Toshio Shiratori|en|Toshio Shiratori|qid=Q717308|lt=Toshio Shiratori|1=Toshio Shiratori}} was the reason. Tomita wrote down the contents of his conversations with the emperor in his diaries and notebooks in detail. He left 12 diaries (1975–1986) and some 20 notebooks (1986–1997). According to the memorandum, the emperor Hirohito expressed his anger and strong displeasure in 1988 at the decision made by Yasukuni Shrine to include [[:en:Class A War Criminal|Class A War Criminal]]s in the list of war dead honored there by saying, "At some point, Class-A criminals became enshrined, including Matsuoka and Shiratori. I heard Tsukuba acted cautiously", Tsukuba is believed to refer to Fujimaro Tsukuba, the former chief Yasukuni priest at the time, who decided not to enshrine the war criminals despite receiving, in 1966, the list of war dead compiled by the government containing their names. "What's on the mind of Matsudaira's son, who is the current head priest?". "Matsudaira had a strong wish for peace, but the child didn't know the parent's heart. That's why I have not visited the shrine since. This is my heart", Matsudaira is believed to refer to Yoshitami Matsudaira, who was the grand steward of Imperial Household immediately after the end of World War II. His son, Nagayoshi, succeeded Fujimaro Tsukuba as the chief priest of Yasukuni and he decided to enshrine the war criminals in 1978.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20060721a1.html | title=Hirohito visits to Yasukuni stopped over war criminals | date=July 21, 2006 |qq=}}</ref> [[:ja:松平永芳|Nagayoshi Matsudaira]] had died the previous year (July 10, 2005), which is speculated as a reason for the release of the memo. For journalist Masanori Yamaguchi, who analyzed the "memo" and comments made by the emperor in his first-ever press conference in 1975, his evasive and opaque attitude about his own responsibility for the war and the fact that he said that the bombing of Hiroshima "{{ill|Shikata ga nai|en|Shikata ga nai|qid=Q1717210|lt=could not be helped|1=Shikata ga nai}}",<ref>"-Does your majesty feel responsibility for the war itself, including the opening of hostilities ? -I can't answer that kind of question because I haven't thoroughly studied the literature in this field, and so I don't really appreciate the nuances of your words." H. Bix, ''Hirohito and the making of modern Japan'', 200, p.676</ref> could mean that he was afraid that the enshrinement would reignite the debate over his own responsibility for the war.<ref name="JT1" /> Another commentator stated that there are three immediate impacts of the memo.<ref>http://www.janjan.jp/government/0607/0607228359/1.php {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024|qq=}}</ref> Firstly, the explanation of the suspension of the imperial visit offered by the right is no longer sustainable. Those on the right of Japanese politics had attributed the reason for the emperor's suspension of visits to the emergence of controversy over constitutional validity of the visit by the prime minister or the emperor in regard to the separation of state and religion. This claim is no longer valid in the light of the revelation. Secondly, Yasukuni and its lay organisation Izokukai probably have to make alterations to their stance somewhat. Both organisations have clearly expressed their wish for a visit by the current emperor. Recent rulings by the Supreme Court have also indicated that visits by the prime minister or the emperor are constitutional. However, it is now clear that the controversy over the enshrinement of class A war criminals has to be resolved. Moreover, though the emperor is the highest authority of Shinto, he does not exercise direct control of any Shinto shrine including Yasukuni. However, Yasukuni ideology is clearly in favour of the pre-war arrangement in which the emperor was the official head of Shinto. Thirdly, the revelation clearly shifts the focus of the controversy to the enshrinement of class A war criminals, meaning that the issue of the separation between the state and the church is no longer the main focus. The public opinion is split between those on the left who advocate for the removal and those on the right who nonetheless object to the removal. ==New memorial== On October 28, 2005, the {{ill|Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)|en|Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)|qid=Q232595|lt=Liberal Democratic Party (LDP)|1=Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)}} and the {{ill|Democratic Party of Japan|en|Democratic Party of Japan|qid=Q200314|lt=Democratic Party of Japan (DPJ)|1=Democratic Party of Japan}} agreed to set up a cross-party "group for pushing forward the establishment of a national memorial facility" to bring about the foundation of a secular war memorial dedicated only to "ordinary" soldiers. This would replace Yasukuni Shrine as the home of Japan's war dead. The group was set to meet for the first time on November 9, 2005. ==Yasukuni documentary controversy== {{Main|Yasukuni (film)|qq=}} In March 2008, a group of lawmakers from the {{ill|Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)|en|Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)|qid=Q232595|lt=Liberal Democratic Party|1=Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)}}, featuring {{ill|Tomomi Inada|en|Tomomi Inada|qid=Q1654408|lt=Tomomi Inada|1=Tomomi Inada}}, called for boycott of a {{ill|Yasukuni (film)|en|Yasukuni (film)|qid=Q850790|lt=documentary made on Yasukuni|1=Yasukuni (film)}} by Chinese-born director Ying Li about the links between the shrine and right-wing movements such as the {{ill|Uyoku dantai|en|Uyoku dantai|qid=Q181924|lt=Uyoku dantai|1=Uyoku dantai}}. Those lawmakers had asked for a preview on the ground that the movie could be "anti-Japanese".<ref>''Dancing with the Devil over Yasukuni'', http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fl20080408zg.html</ref> The {{ill|Directors Guild of Japan|en|Directors Guild of Japan|qid=Q4117717|lt=Directors Guild of Japan|1=Directors Guild of Japan}} expressed apprehension about the possible infringement of freedom of expression and as a result of the politicians' protests, only about 10 theaters would screen the movie, and none in Tokyo.<ref>''More than 10 cinemas will screen Yasukuni'', http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20080405a1.html, ''No Tokyo theater will show Yasukuni film'', http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20080401f1.html</ref> == Attacks == === Cyber attacks === Beginning with Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi's annual visits to the shrine, the shrine experienced regular cyber attacks in the early 2000s. These attacks have been found to originate from Chinese servers in China and involve Chinese-language spam with viruses. These attacks serve two functions. They spam the Yasukuni shrine webpage to overwhelm them and basically prevent them from being accessible. Some of these attacks reached as high as 15,000 pings per second. Cyber attacks against the shrine have also included sending mass emails that appear to have originated from the shrine with viruses to third parties.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2005-01-07|title=Yasukuni Shrine Web site crippled in cyber war|url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2005/01/07/national/yasukuni-shrine-web-site-crippled-in-cyber-war/|access-date=2021-05-18|website={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|language=en-US|qq=}}</ref> === December 2011 arson attack === On December 26, 2011, a man attempted to burn down a 13-meter high gate column at Yasukuni. The incident was captured on video and the fire quickly put out by security staff. Japanese police gained an arrest warrant for a 37-year-old Chinese man, who had been arrested earlier the same month for throwing Molotov cocktails at the Japanese embassy in {{ill|Seoul|en|Seoul|qid=Q8684|lt=Seoul|1=Seoul}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/nn20120119b4.html|title=Warrant issued for Yasukuni arsonist January 19, 2012|qq=}}</ref> The man, Liu Qiang, served a 10-month sentence in South Korea for the embassy attack. Japan formally asked South Korea to extradite him, and China informally requested repatriation of their citizen. Liu stated that he was motivated by "antihumanitarian acts by militaristic Japan," and said that his (South Korean) maternal grandmother had been a {{ill|Comfort women|en|Comfort women|qid=Q713540|lt=sex slave for Japanese soldiers|1=Comfort women}} during the war and his great-grandfather had been tortured to death for protesting.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/nn20121201a7.html|title=Yasukuni fire suspect spouts gripe December 1, 2012|qq=}}</ref> On January 3, 2013, the Seoul High Court declined Japan's extradition request, and on the following day Liu returned to China.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/nn20130105a9.html|title=Yasukuni arson suspect leaves Seoul for China; Abe issues regret January 5, 2012|qq=}}</ref> === November 2015 bombing === On November 23, 2015, at around 10 am, a restroom near the shrine's southern entrance was damaged by a bomb explosion.<ref>[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2015/11/23/national/crime-legal/explosion-damages-toilet-at-japans-controversial-yasukuni-shrine/ Explosion damages restroom at Tokyo's Yasukuni Shrine November 23, 2015] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160501103117/http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2015/11/23/national/crime-legal/explosion-damages-toilet-at-japans-controversial-yasukuni-shrine |date=May 1, 2016 |qq=}} ''[[:en:Japan Times|Japan Times]]'' Retrieved December 11, 2015</ref> Chon Chang-han, a South Korean man, was arrested on December 9.<ref>[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2015/12/10/national/crime-legal/south-korean-admitted-setting-explosives-yasukuni-shrine-police-sources/ South Korean 'admitted' to setting explosives at Yasukuni Shrine: police sources December 10, 2015] ''[[:en:Japan Times|Japan Times]]'' Retrieved December 11, 2015</ref> == Visits by media figures == On January 7, 2019, the official {{ill|Twitter|en|Twitter|qid=Q918|lt=Twitter|1=Twitter}} account for [[:en:Creatures (company)|Creatures]], one of the co-owners of the ''{{ill|Pokémon|en|Pokémon|qid=Q864|lt=Pokémon|1=Pokémon}}'' franchise, posted an image depicting multiple employees visiting Yasukuni Shrine. The tweet was deleted shortly after due to backlash from Chinese and South Korean Twitter users, which included calls for a boycott.<ref name="Ashcraft 2019">{{cite news |last1=Ashcraft |first1=Brian |title=Pokémon Studio Criticized After Visiting Controversial Shrine |url=https://kotaku.com/pokemon-studio-criticized-after-visiting-controversial-1831601511 |access-date=20 July 2023 |work=Kotaku |publisher=G/O Media |date=9 January 2019 |ref=Ashcraft 2019|qq=}}</ref><ref name="Hoffer 2019">{{cite news |last1=Hoffer |first1=Christian |title=Pokemon Studio Under Fire For Visiting Controversial Shrine |url=https://comicbook.com/gaming/news/pokemon-creatures-inc-yasukuni-shrine-controversy/ |access-date=20 July 2023 |work=Comic Book Gaming |date=9 January 2019 |ref=Hoffer 2019|qq=}}</ref><ref name="Loveridge 2019">{{cite news |last1=Loveridge |first1=Lynzee |title=Pokémon's Creatures, Inc. in Hot Water Over Visit to Controversial Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.animenewsnetwork.com/interest/2019-01-10/pokemon-creatures-inc-in-hot-water-over-visit-to-controversial-yasukuni-shrine/.141891 |access-date=20 July 2023 |work=Anime News Network |publisher=Kadokawa Corporation |date=10 January 2019 |ref=Loveridge 2019|qq=}}</ref> On 11 February 2021, Japanese voice actress {{ill|Ai Kayano|en|Ai Kayano|qid=Q1187078|lt=Ai Kayano|1=Ai Kayano}} claimed that she had taken a trip to visit Yasukuni Shrine, located near her workplace, which upset many citizens of nations that Japan had victimized during its imperialistic era. She apologized afterwards, saying that she was unaware of the controversy on the enshrinement of war criminals in the shrine. A few months after her visit, amid significant pressure from Chinese fans, some Chinese video games such as ''{{ill|Azur Lane|en|Azur Lane|qid=Q42404311|lt=Azur Lane|1=Azur Lane}}'' and ''{{ill|Arknights|en|Arknights|qid=Q63789030|lt=Arknights|1=Arknights}}'' had removed her voice work from their games in the Chinese server.<ref>[https://www.scmp.com/week-asia/politics/article/3121883/japan-voice-actress-ai-kayano-sparks-anger-chinas-social-media Japanese anime star Ai Kayano sparks anger on China's social media after visit to controversial Yasukuni Shrine] ''[[:en:SCMP|SCMP]]'' Retrieved February 16, 2021</ref> ==See also== * {{ill|Controversies regarding the role of the Emperor of Japan|en|Controversies regarding the role of the Emperor of Japan|qid=Q211225|lt=Controversies regarding the role of the Emperor of Japan|1=Controversies regarding the role of the Emperor of Japan}} ==References== {{reflist|qq=}}
